skill.name: cursor
skill.description: Use Cursor AI editor CLI commands to open projects, compare files, manage windows, configure extensions, and locate settings.
skill.summary: Guides agents through common Cursor AI editor CLI workflows.
skill.author: Dicklesworthstone

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many Markdown code spans and fences as Ruby backtick execution, but the file contains documentation for Cursor CLI usage rather than executable Ruby code. The confirmed risk is that the skill asks an agent to run external editor commands, install or disable extensions, change git editor settings, and reference local settings and SSH configuration paths, so publication should include a command-execution warning.

medium_findings.title: Agent-Guided External CLI Operations
medium_findings.description: The skill intentionally instructs agents to run Cursor CLI and git commands. This is legitimate for an editor-control skill, but it can open arbitrary local paths, alter global git editor settings, install or disable extensions, and consume piped input when a user approves those commands.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are clearly present as Cursor and git CLI examples, and they match the stated purpose of the skill. There is no evidence of hidden execution, but command approval and path review remain important.

medium_findings.title: Local Configuration Path Exposure
medium_findings.description: The skill documents Cursor settings, keybindings, user data directories, extension directories, and the SSH remote configuration path. These are expected references, but agents could read or modify sensitive configuration if a user asks them to act on these paths.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file paths are explicit and include the SSH configuration path. The skill does not tell the agent to read secrets or exfiltrate data, so the concern is elevated but contextual.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Static False Positives
low_findings.description: The scanner reported Ruby or shell backtick execution across Markdown inline code and fenced command examples. No Ruby code, command substitution, or automatic execution path is present in the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed file is Markdown documentation, and the flagged backticks delimit code formatting or fenced examples. The semantic context does not show Ruby execution.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static False Positive
low_findings.description: The scanner reported a weak cryptographic algorithm at the description line. No cryptographic algorithm, hash operation, cipher configuration, or related code appears at that location.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is plain front matter describing Cursor editor control. I found no cryptographic API or algorithm reference in the reviewed skill content.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Command Invocation Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill provides many commands that an agent may ask to run through the shell, including opening files, configuring git, managing extensions, and changing Cursor runtime directories.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are explicit and numerous, which confirms the pattern. They are not malicious by themselves, but they require user review before execution.

dangerous_patterns.title: Sensitive SSH Configuration Reference
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill points users to the SSH remote configuration file. This is useful for remote development, but the path can contain hostnames, usernames, aliases, and identity file references.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The SSH config path is directly documented. There is no instruction to extract or transmit it, so the risk depends on follow-up actions.

content.value_statement: Developers often need precise editor actions while working with Claude, Codex, or Claude Code. This skill gives clear Cursor CLI commands for opening files, comparing changes, managing windows, and handling extensions.
actual_capabilities: Open files, folders, and projects in Cursor from terminal commands.
Open files at specific line and column positions.
Compare two files using Cursor diff mode.
Control new window, reused window, and wait-mode behavior.
List, install, uninstall, and disable Cursor extensions.
Locate Cursor settings, keybindings, extension directories, and SSH remote configuration.
limitations: Requires the Cursor CLI to be installed and available on the local system.
Does not automate the Cursor graphical interface directly.
Extension installation depends on valid extension identifiers and marketplace access.
Remote development setup still requires correct SSH configuration and user approval.

use_cases.title: Open Review Targets Quickly
use_cases.description: Open a project file at the exact line that needs review or editing.

use_cases.title: Compare Local File Changes
use_cases.description: Launch Cursor diff mode to inspect two files before accepting changes.

use_cases.title: Prepare Cursor Workspaces
use_cases.description: Open folders, add workspace paths, and manage extensions during setup.

prompt_templates.title: Open This Project
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner workflow for opening the current repository.
prompt_templates.prompt: Open the current project in Cursor using the safest CLI command. Explain the command before asking to run it.

prompt_templates.title: Open a File Location
prompt_templates.scenario: Open a specific source file at a requested line.
prompt_templates.prompt: Open the file I name in Cursor at the line and column I provide. Confirm the path first.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Two Files
prompt_templates.scenario: Use Cursor diff mode for review.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Cursor CLI diff mode to compare these two files. Show the command and wait for my approval.

prompt_templates.title: Configure Cursor Workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced workflow for editor, extensions, and workspace settings.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help configure Cursor as my git editor and review extension commands. Do not change global settings without explicit confirmation.

output_examples.input: Open the current repository in Cursor.
output_examples.output: The assistant identifies the current folder, proposes the Cursor command, and asks for approval before launching the editor.

output_examples.input: Compare two implementation files before I merge them.
output_examples.output: The assistant prepares a Cursor diff action for the two paths and explains which file appears on each side.

output_examples.input: Help me make Cursor my git editor.
output_examples.output: The assistant explains the global git editor setting, notes that it affects future git commands, and requests confirmation.

best_practices: Review every proposed command before allowing the agent to run it.
Use relative project paths when possible to avoid opening unrelated files.
Confirm extension identifiers and global configuration changes before applying them.
anti_patterns: Do not let an agent open or edit sensitive configuration without a clear task.
Do not install extensions from untrusted publishers or unclear identifiers.
Do not change global git settings during a temporary or shared environment session.

faq.question: What does this skill do?
faq.answer: It provides Cursor CLI commands for opening files, folders, diffs, extensions, settings, and remote development references.

faq.question: Does it require Cursor to be installed?
faq.answer: Yes. The local Cursor command must be installed and available in the terminal path.

faq.question: Can it edit files by itself?
faq.answer: No. It opens files in Cursor. Any actual edits depend on user actions or separate agent commands.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill is legitimate, but it guides external commands and references local configuration paths.

faq.question: Can it manage Cursor extensions?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents commands to list, install, uninstall, and disable extensions.

faq.question: Is the SSH configuration reference malicious?
faq.answer: No evidence found. It is a normal remote development reference, but users should protect that file.
